entities.description: A 2006 Level 4 TEI-in-Libraries encoding of a later Oxford University Press carrier preserving Furnivall's revised 1889 textual state. It includes the edition title page; introduction; 1889 contents; target Books I-II on printed pages 1-25; the separately appended Spheres and Planets on page 26; and Gill's notes on pages 27-28. It omits the revised edition's glossary on pages 29-31.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item ANZ4356
notes: The PROFILEDESC declares eng, but the target medieval work is Middle English with brief embedded Latin; modern English belongs to edition paratext.
The XML SOURCEDESC specifies the N. Trübner revised 1889 edition, whereas the encoded title page gives a later Oxford University Press London-New York-Toronto imprint without a reprint date.

rights.basis: The XML header states that where applicable the item is subject to copyright and that other distribution restrictions may apply; it supplies no item-specific open license.
rights.component: University of Michigan electronic encoding and editorial markup
notes: This provider statement is kept separate from the Unlicense covering the pinned CME repository artifact.

entities.description: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's Latin quintessence treatise, distinguished here from manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, facsimiles, encodings, and the separately appended Spheres and Planets.
entities.label: The Book of Quinte Essence (abstract Middle English work)
notes: The incipit's Hermes attribution is legendary. No translator is named and no physical Latin exemplar for this version is identified.

rights.basis: The anonymous verbal work is attested in fifteenth-century manuscripts and was first published in 1866; this component excludes modern editions, encodings, metadata, and photography.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English verbal work
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where historical copyright terms have expired
notes: This is a component- and jurisdiction-specific research assessment, not a worldwide guarantee about every reproduction or contribution.

entities.description: The two-book Latin work on quintessence composed by John of Rupescissa in 1351-1352: Book I develops the alchemical theory and manufacture of quintessence, and Book II presents medical applications. It is the intellectual source work behind the Middle English translation traditions, not an identified physical exemplar in this chain.
entities.label: John of Rupescissa, Liber de consideratione quintae essentiae omnium rerum

rights.basis: John of Rupescissa composed the source work in 1351-1352; this component excludes modern editions, translations, and reproductions.
rights.component: Underlying fourteenth-century Latin verbal work
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where historical copyright terms have expired

entities.description: A short anonymous Middle English astrological account of the celestial spheres and planets at Sloane MS 73, f. 26. Furnivall appended it to the revised edition on page 26 in 1889; it is modeled as a separate related work, not part of the target's intrinsic two-book structure.
entities.label: The Spheres and Planets

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Book of Quinte Essence; or, The Fifth Being; That Is to Say, Man's Heaven, Early English Text Society, Original Series 16 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1866 [revised 1889]), viii + 31 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 16
bibliographic.title: The Book of Quinte Essence; or, The Fifth Being; That Is to Say, Man's Heaven

entities.description: The revised state underlying the U-M encoding. Its title page retains the 1866 imprint with a bracketed Revised, 1889 statement; Furnivall's postscript says the Contents on pages vii-viii and The Spheres on page 26 were then added. The target occupies pages 1-25, Gill's notes pages 27-28, and the glossary pages 29-31.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's revised edition of The Book of Quinte Essence (1889)
notes: Furnivall identifies Sloane MS 73 as base text, credits Edmund Brock with reading proof against it, and says Harley MS 853 was collated only for a few passages requiring elucidation.
The complete revised scan was recovered from Wikimedia Commons after its Internet Archive/Digital Library of India source item became access-restricted.

rights.basis: The revised edition was published in 1889 and is publicly identified as public domain by Wikimedia Commons; all named nineteenth-century contributors died long before current copyright terms.
rights.component: Furnivall's revised 1889 edition text, introduction, notes, and typography

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Book of Quinte Essence; or, The Fifth Being (London: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1866), Early English Text Society, Original Series 16.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 16
bibliographic.title: The Book of Quinte Essence; or, The Fifth Being

entities.description: The first printed edition and prior state of Furnivall's text. Direct title-page inspection establishes 1866. It contains the target on pages 1-25 but lacks the 1889 Contents and Spheres additions; it proceeds from the target's ending to Gill's notes and the glossary.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's first edition of The Book of Quinte Essence (1866)
notes: Internet Archive metadata gives 1889 despite the inspected 1866 title page; that provider date is preserved as contradicted provider metadata, not adopted as the edition date.

rights.basis: The inspected edition was published in London in 1866 and all named contributors died long before current copyright terms.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1866 first-edition text, introduction, notes, and typography

localCopies.label: British Library 2025/26 digitisation and reproduction rate card
notes: This is a service price document supporting the request route; it contains no part of the target work or manuscript.

For a complete-object facsimile, request covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, every unfoliated flyleaf and blank, both sides of every folio in the current object, edges where available, final pastedown, and any detached matter. Ask staff to confirm the full current foliation and image inventory because the interim record does not provide it.

Associate the target separately with ff. 10r-25v and The Spheres and Planets with f. 26r-v. A request limited to those leaves would be a partial work-focused reproduction, not a complete manuscript facsimile.
Delivery does not itself grant republication rights; request written permission and exact repository/publication terms separately.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and contractual delivery context

entities.description: The fifteenth-century English base witness directly transcribed by Furnivall. The target is the item cataloged as Hermes Trismegistus, De quintis essentiis, at ff. 10-25; Furnivall's edition milestones run from 10a through 25b, interpreted as ff. 10r-25v. The separately cataloged astrology text The Spheres and Planets follows at f. 26.

holding.notes: The target occupies ff. 10r-25v under the current record's ff. 10-25 description; Spheres and Planets is a separate item at f. 26.

entities.description: A collateral later witness to the condensed Middle English version. The current catalog identifies the liber quintae essentiae at ff. 66r-73v and records its ending date as 22 August 1589. Furnivall says this copy was collated only for a few passages requiring elucidation, so it is not the base witness or a direct ancestor of the whole edition.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 853, ff. 66r-73v
notes: No public complete manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.
The current dated ending supersedes Furnivall and Bond's rough about-1600 estimate.

physicalDescription.extent: f. 1* + 152 folios, with four unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and five at the end; target ff. 66r-73v
notes: Written in Gothic cursive and produced in England.
The post-1600 British Museum binding was renewed on 28 September 1966.

Halversen, The Consideration of Quintessence: An Edition of a Middle English Translation of John of Rupescissa's Liber de Consideratione de Quintae Essentiae Omnium Rerum with Introduction, Notes and Commentary (PhD diss., Michigan State University, 1998), xii + 484 pp., doi:10.25335/bav5-r656.
bibliographic.title: The Consideration of Quintessence

entities.description: A modern critical edition and study of a different Middle English textual state, based principally on Ferguson MS 205 and Sloane MS 353. Its witness census and prior-edition discussion analyze Sloane MS 73 and Harley MS 853 as the condensed second version. It supports the lineage claims but is not an ancestor of Furnivall's edition or the U-M encoding.
entities.label: Marguerite A. Halversen, The Consideration of Quintessence (1998 dissertation)

next_steps: Submit separate British Library complete-object digitisation enquiries and request item-specific quotations.

next_steps: Audit every Furnivall variant note marked MS. Harl. against the complete first and revised editions.
Compare those passages with an authorized Harley MS 853 facsimile if obtained.

next_steps: Compare any alternate U-M delivery files or archived documentation.
Ask U-M whether the page-range selection was intentional.

reviewNotes: Hermes Trismegistus is an inherited legendary attribution in the Middle English incipit, not the accepted author of either the Latin source or this anonymous English translation-adaptation.
The Spheres and Planets is a separate medieval text from Sloane MS 73, f. 26, appended to the edition in 1889; it is not an intrinsic third book of The Book of Quinte Essence.

The revised edition's glossary on pages 29-31 is listed in the encoded contents but omitted from the XML. The encoding relation therefore covers selected portions of the revised volume, not the full printed carrier.

Halversen gives Sloane MS 73, ff. 11-25v, while Furnivall's folio milestones and the current British Library record begin the target at f. 10. The current record and edition milestones control the principal locator, with the discrepancy preserved as an open question.
Internet Archive labels its first-edition scan 1889, but direct inspection of the title page establishes 1866 and the volume lacks the additions that Furnivall expressly says were made in 1889.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item ANZ4356. Its source description identifies Frederick J. Furnivall's revised 1889 edition, and its inclusion of the 1889 Contents and separately added Spheres and Planets confirms that revised textual state, although the encoded Oxford University Press title page leaves the exact later carrier or reprint unresolved. Furnivall's target text on pages 1-25 directly transcribes British Library Sloane MS 73, ff. 10r-25v, with proof read against that manuscript by Edmund Brock; Harley MS 853, ff. 66r-73v, was consulted only for a few passages. The Sloane and Harley texts represent a condensed Middle English translation-adaptation of John of Rupescissa's 1351-1352 Latin Liber de consideratione quintae essentiae. The 1866 first edition is an earlier state, not the U-M encoding's immediate source, and Halversen's 1998 dissertation is supporting scholarship rather than an ancestor. Complete scans of both Furnivall states and supporting scholarship are cached. No authorized public complete facsimile of Sloane MS 73 or Harley MS 853 was verified on 2026-07-15; current British Library catalog, onsite consultation, paid photography, and permission routes are recorded instead.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
catalogSummary.date: Fifteenth century; attested circa 1460-1470 in Sloane MS 73
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of translation and dialect region unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Book of Quinte Essence

catalogingSubject.label: The Book of Quinte Essence
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's Latin Liber de consideratione quintae essentiae, represented in the encoding by its two intrinsic books on edition pages 1-25. The record does not treat Hermes Trismegistus's inherited legendary attribution as modern authorship. It retains Furnivall's title page, introduction, 1889 contents, and C. H. Gill's chemistry notes as zero-count edition paratexts; it models The Spheres and Planets on page 26 as a separately appended related medieval work from Sloane MS 73, f. 26, not as Book III or an intrinsic section of The Book of Quinte Essence. The revised edition's glossary on pages 29-31 is listed in the encoded contents but absent from the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Edition title page
contentParts.summary: Furnivall edition title and publication matter retained as print paratext.

contentParts.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's introduction
contentParts.summary: Editorial introduction dated 16 May 1866, with an 1889 postscript identifying newly added contents and Spheres material.

contentParts.label: Contents added in 1889
notes: The contents list promises a glossary on page 29, but the XML stops after Gill's notes on page 28.
contentParts.summary: Two-page editorial contents list covering Books I-II, Spheres and Planets, Gill's notes, and the printed glossary.

contentParts.label: The Book of Quinte Essence
contentParts.summary: The one cataloged Middle English prose translation-adaptation, divided into two books.

contentParts.label: Book I
contentParts.summary: Theory, preparation, and extraction of quintessence, including repeated distillation of wine and preparations involving gold, antimony, blood, and the four elements.

contentParts.label: Book II
contentParts.summary: Sixteen numbered medicines and additional therapeutic directions applying quintessence to rejuvenation, disease, poison, fevers, and pestilence.

contentParts.label: The Spheres and Planets
contentParts.summary: A separately appended Middle English astrological text transcribed from the following manuscript folio and added to the edition in 1889; it is not an intrinsic book of The Book of Quinte Essence.

contentParts.label: Notes on the Chemistry of the Text
contentParts.summary: C. H. Gill's nineteenth-century explanatory and critical notes on the treatise's chemistry.

assertion.notes: The repository encoding follows the revised textual state, not the unrevised 1866 state.

extent.description: One predominantly Middle English prose work in two intrinsic books on revised-edition pages 1-25, with brief embedded Latin citations and a Latin closing caution. The title page, introduction, contents, separately appended Spheres and Planets, and Gill notes are retained as zero-count parts; the edition's listed glossary is absent from the XML.

formStatements.description: Continuous instructional and medical prose in two books

assertion.notes: Book II applies quintessence preparations to a sequence of illnesses and therapeutic aims.

notes: The current record is partial because the translation's precise origin, Latin exemplar, U-M carrier state, and complete-manuscript image availability remain unresolved.
Astrology is not assigned as a facet of the cataloged work: the astrological Spheres and Planets belongs to a separately appended related work.
Modern-English introduction, contents, and chemical notes are carrier paratext and therefore do not add English to the medieval work's catalog-summary language facet.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's fourteenth-century Latin treatise on quintessence. Book I explains the theory and manufacture of a purified, repeatedly distilled substance associated with aqua ardens and describes extracting or combining quintessences from gold, antimony, blood, and other materials. Book II applies the substance to rejuvenation and remedies for leprosy, palsy, fevers, gout, frenzy, poison, pestilence, and other conditions.

The medieval work has two intrinsic books. The encoded carrier also contains a title page, Furnivall's introduction, contents added in 1889, the unrelated appended Spheres and Planets from the next Sloane folio, and C. H. Gill's modern chemical notes. The printed glossary listed in the contents is not encoded.

Hermes Trismegistus is retained only as a legendary attribution inherited by the incipit. The Latin source work is attributed to John of Rupescissa by modern scholarship; neither figure is converted into the author of the anonymous Middle English translation-adaptation. Edition paratexts and the separately appended astrological text are not counted as medieval-work items.

publication.embeddedTitle: The Book of Quinte Essence — or The Fifth Being; — That is to say, Man's Heaven. — A tretice in englisch breuely drawe out of þe book of quintis eessencijs in latyn, þat hermys þe prophete and kyng of Egipt, after þe flood of Noe fadir of philosophris, hadde by reuelacioun of an aungil of god to him sende.

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's fourteenth-century Latin treatise on quintessence. Book I explains the theory and manufacture of a purified, repeatedly distilled substance associated with aqua ardens and describes extracting or combining quintessences from gold, antimony, blood, and other materials. Book II applies the substance to rejuvenation and remedies for leprosy, palsy, fevers, gout, frenzy, poison, pestilence, and other conditions.
